questions.scenario: A beginner says the compiler is too strict because it complains before the program runs. Which response best matches the article?

options.text: That strictness is part of moving some memory and state bugs from runtime into compile time.
The compiler is only checking whitespace and formatting.
The correct response is to use unwrap everywhere until the errors disappear.
Rust is strict because Cargo cannot run tests.

questions.explanation: Rusts early cost is that it asks you to describe ownership, mutation, and failure more honestly before the program runs.

questions.scenario: Which Rust choices from the article cost something early but buy maintainability later?
questions.explanation: Ownership, error types, and Cargo conventions all require learning, but improve feedback and consistency.

questions.scenario: What is the best way to understand no garbage collector in the article?

options.text: It is a design constraint that gives Rust predictable cleanup points and makes it fit constrained environments.
It proves garbage-collected languages are bad.
It means Rust developers manually call free in normal code.
It means heap memory never exists in Rust.

questions.explanation: No built-in GC is about predictability and placement, not moral superiority or manual free in normal code.
